text: The surgical setup for a completely laparoscopic colectomy is shown, featuring a twelve millimeter port supraumbilical, a twelve millimeter port through the ileostomy site, and two five millimeter ports arranged in a diamond configuration. The procedure begins at the left pelvic brim with a lateral to medial approach.
The surgeon uses electrocautery to dissect along the white line, maintaining a bloodless plane. The left ureter is visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon navigates the retroperitoneal plane along the descending colon, with the left peritoneal reflection visible. The white line remains with the patient, guiding the dissection as it progresses cephalad over the kidney towards the spleen, which is visible at the top of the screen.

The surgeon mobilizes the proximal descending colon off the retroperitoneum, moving the plane medially closer to the colon. The distal transverse colon is then mobilized, with the option to enter the lesser sac from below, leaving the omentum with the patient, or from above, taking the omentum with the colon. The splenic flexure is fully mobilized.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealing device to manage the omentum, ensuring the left side is fully mobilized to the midline before proceeding to the right side.

The surgeon scores the peritoneum around the base of the cecum and the terminal ileal mesentery, using a lateral to medial approach to mobilize the ascending colon from the retroperitoneum. The white line remains visible, indicating the correct plane of dissection.
The surgeon uses a lateral to medial approach to preserve the ileocolic pedicle, applying careful traction and counter traction to clearly visualize the dissection plane.

The surgeon fully mobilizes the right colon up to the duodenum, visible at the bottom of the screen. Cold cutting is performed near the duodenum to avoid cautery. The hepatic flexure is then mobilized, with careful identification and protection of the duodenum during dissection, sweeping tissues downward.
The surgeon identifies the retroperitoneal plane around the hepatic flexure, ensuring the colon remains vascularized during the rectal dissection in the pelvis.

The surgeon begins on the left side, entering the presacral space and extending the dissection medially and distally. The procedure then shifts to the right side, where the ureter is identified, and the rectum is elevated. The right parorectal peritoneum is scored, connecting with the previous left-side dissection. The surgeon continues cutting along the marked line, working further down in the pelvis on the right side of the rectum, moving from lateral to anterior and towards the pelvic floor. The shiny fascia over the pelvic floor becomes visible as the dissection approaches the muscle.

In female patients, the surgeon suspends the uterus using a Keith needle through the fundus and employs a sponge stick in the vagina for retraction, aiding in the identification of the rectovaginal septum. Before stapling, the surgeon performs a digital check to confirm the dissection reaches the top of the anal canal and ensures the staple line does not include the back wall of the vagina.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealing device to divide the mesentery, performing the division at a convenient location without concern for cancerous tissue.

The surgeon makes a cruciate incision in the anterior and posterior fascia at the right lower quadrant site, separating the muscle fibers. The entire specimen is extracted through this site, keeping the ileocolic pedicle intact.
The surgeon proceeds to transect the bowel and create a pouch. A drain is left in the pelvis, and one of the five millimeter port sites is repurposed as a drain site.
The patient has a ten millimeter incision just above the umbilicus, with two additional small incisions nearby to accommodate her navel ring.

The surgeon completes the mobilization of the colon through a single incision, bringing the mesentery out through the ileostomy site. The ileocolic artery is preserved while transecting the terminal ileum. A standard fifteen-centimeter J pouch is created and repositioned into the abdomen, followed by the formation of a circular stapled anastomosis. The dissection is performed deep in the pelvis.

The surgeon ensures the mesentery lays straight before firing the stapler, with the camera positioned over the duodenum and retroperitoneum on the right side. The anastomosis is created, and an ileostomy is brought up.
